behaviors.description: Log entries with the same trace ID are grouped together regardless of which microservice emitted them.
behaviors.justification: Trace ID grouping is deterministic logic operating on in-memory data structures. The real failure would be incorrect grouping logic (e.g., case sensitivity, whitespace handling, or wrong grouping key). A Unit test providing multiple entries with identical trace IDs from different service sources and verifying they appear in a single group catches this failure without needing external I/O.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test: create 5 log entries with the same trace ID from 3 different microservices, plus 3 entries with different trace IDs. Assert that entries are grouped into exactly 4 groups (one per unique trace ID) and that the group for the shared trace ID contains exactly 5 entries.

behaviors.description: The timeline view orders entries by timestamp, correctly handling clock skew of up to 500ms between services.
behaviors.justification: This is a sorting and tolerance-validation behavior on in-memory data. The real failure would be misordering due to clock skew (e.g., not accounting for the 500ms window, off-by-one in comparisons, or timezone bugs). A Unit test with entries having intentionally skewed timestamps (100ms, 250ms, 500ms offsets) verifies the sorting logic handles clock skew correctly without needing real inter-service time measurements.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test: create 10 log entries from different services with timestamps spanning 600ms. Service A is on time (T=0), Service B is 250ms behind (T=-250ms), Service C is 100ms ahead (T=+100ms). Assert that timeline orders by actual timestamp and correctly resolves clock skew within 500ms tolerance. Include boundary case where skew is exactly 500ms.

behaviors.description: A latency spike (p99 above 3x the baseline) is detected and flagged in the anomaly report.
behaviors.justification: Spike detection is a deterministic statistical calculation (p99 > 3 * baseline_value). The real failure would be incorrect p99 computation, wrong threshold comparison, or off-by-one errors. A Unit test providing latency datasets with known p99 values (2x baseline, exactly 3x, and 4x) would verify the calculation is correct. No real traffic data or ML is needed—the threshold logic is deterministic.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test with three latency datasets: (1) latencies where p99 = 2.5x baseline (should not flag), (2) latencies where p99 = 3.0x baseline (boundary), (3) latencies where p99 = 3.5x baseline (should flag). Assert that only datasets 2 and 3 produce anomaly flags in the report.

behaviors.description: Malformed JSON log entries are skipped without crashing the pipeline, and the count of skipped entries is reported.
behaviors.justification: While JSON parsing can be tested at Unit level, the critical failure here is pipeline resilience—continuing through mixed valid/invalid data. Mocking the JSON parser would hide whether error handling is actually in place (try-catch wrapping the real parser). The real failure would be: pipeline crashes mid-stream, entries are lost/corrupted, or skipped count is wrong. Integration test of the full parsing pipeline with mixed data catches this; Unit test of the parser alone does not.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test: pass a sequence of 20 log entries to the ingestion pipeline: 15 valid JSON, 3 with syntax errors (missing braces, trailing commas), 2 with schema violations (missing required fields). Assert: (1) pipeline completes without exception, (2) 15 valid entries reach output, (3) skipped count = 5, (4) no partial or corrupted entries appear in output, (5) subsequent valid entries after errors are processed correctly.

behaviors.description: The tool handles a burst of 10,000 log entries per second without dropping entries or falling behind.
behaviors.justification: This behavior depends on real I/O subsystem performance and OS resource constraints (buffer limits, file descriptors, memory, kernel queueing). Mocking I/O or using in-memory test data would hide real bottlenecks (e.g., disk I/O latency, network packet loss, buffer overflow, or scheduler contention). The real failure would be entries dropped from queues, unbounded memory growth, or processing lag under sustained load. Only a System test with realistic data sources (files/network) and sustained load reveals these failures.
behaviors.test_description: System test: generate 10,000 entries/sec from a realistic source (log files streamed, socket data, or message queue) for 10 seconds (100k total entries). Instrument the pipeline to measure: (1) queue depth over time, (2) memory usage, (3) entries processed per second. Assert: (1) no entries lost (all 100k appear in output), (2) queue depth does not grow unboundedly, (3) processing rate >= ingestion rate, (4) memory stays under a reasonable bound (e.g., < 1GB for test load).
